item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sun-nuke at the end really differentiates this worm-hole skip from others. Man, what a predictable (in hindsight) but totally random way to go. Upvoted

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article cannot be fully sourced, as we do not know the two sources used to make the image. Thus, it is to be assumed that it's non-compliant, so the image has been removed.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP can be used to access some form of intelligent alien life. Because of that, I propose linking to Project Heimdall, a series that deals with aliens.

Editing this:

Analysis: This expedition marks the first instance in which SCP-2917 has allowed contact with intelligent life, as well as the first instance of the anomaly connecting with a nonterrestrial planet.

To this:

Analysis: This expedition marks the first instance in which SCP-2917 has allowed contact with intelligent life, as well as the first instance of the anomaly connecting with a nonterrestrial planet. Usage of SCP-2917 in the course of Project Heimdall is awaiting approval.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like this Scp. I'm.a sucker for well written tech skips. My only critique is the same for all.these wormhole-like objects. If the Earth and the planet the exit portal is on are moving through space independently, how is the wormhole moving in a way to remain on the same spot on the earth, why is no one thinking these are delibrate constructs?
'Natural' wormholes wouldn't be tethered to a planet like that.
It's like the delorion from back to the future. When they 'drive' into the past or future they should appear in the heartless void of space where Earth hasn't gotten to.yet or left far behind.

item.title: Re: double as
item.content:encoded: Fixed. Thanks!

item.title: double as
item.content:encoded:
In the beginning it says "as as well as retrieval"
Was this a mistake or an unrecorded side effect of being in the proximity of SCP-2917 :)
Just letting you know as to prevent it being downvoted.

+1

item.title: Re: 30th +1
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Seems like it's been a little while since anybody saw this page.

item.title: 30th +1
item.content:encoded: I like it, and it's pretty good for a first SCP. I agree with some other comments that the image used is really cheesy, but everything else more than makes up for it. The exploration log is by far the best part of the document. The "wormhole to other planets" idea is kind of overused, but this manages to make good use of it through using unique imagery and concepts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You kinda faded in and out for a while, where did you go?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought it was great and the perfect amount of subduedness so as not to be over the top wacky with random bullshit while grounded in realism.

for me, something like opening a wormhole portal and finding a barren landscape with too many stars is more impactful and interesting than finding a Gothic-looking cathedral made of beeswax inhabited by dogmen wearing TRON-like armor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm in the midst of a rewrite on the logs. Long story short, I'm spicing up the planets, and in fact separating them from the experimentation on its properties, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ feedback
- Hide whatever

how it maintains it's continued presence

Its.

One (1)

Two (2)

As far as I know, site policy and the general consensus in regards to proper clinical tone permit you to choose one of the two forms of numeral. I might be wrong on this, but at the very least, IMHO it looks and reads sloppy, and it makes little sense for someone reading the document to be ignorant of such a basic spoken word in the English language, if they've already comprehended everything leading up to it.

Analysis of stars suggests

This is vague. Which stars are being analyzed? I recommend changing this to, 'Analysis of the positioning of other celestial bodies located within the deep background of the image,' or something more directly referential to minor objects within the window.

equipped with a standard Environmental Protection suit, in order to determine the cause of relocation events.

It appears as though this 'Subject' category, in all other tests besides this one, the one after it, and E, is merely used to describe what is being thrust into the hole - not its purpose. I would recommend placing these mission objectives into the log, to maintain consistency.

To further establish the causes

You already used the singular of 'cause' in the prior test. I recommend changing this to 'cause(s) to denote an uncertainty in regards to both the quality and quantity of the what that causes relocation events to occur.

, such as the Coalsack Nebula.

This is oddly specific, and feels unnecessary. If the person reading this is an astronomer, they're probably familiar with dark nebulae. If they aren't, they probably won't get anything out of the reference.

Analysis: The tether was considered to be a second object.

I'm not sure what this means.

An icy world

This works, but to improve its clinicality I recommend using more science. Yummy, delicious, fat-free science. Like 'An unidentifed tundra environment dominated by sub-zero precipitation and low-visibility,' something or other.

blood-red

This feels non-clinical. I recommend it this to crimson or just red.

Unlike the barren surface of all previous locations, this world sustains life - the anomaly opens to a forest filled with 20-30m blue and orange bioluminescent organisms superficially resembling trees. It is nighttime, and Barnard's Loop is visible in the sky, with an angular diameter of ~30 degrees.

Just want to say that I like this. This is the shit. This is how you establish location; a few defining characteristics that give the reader's brain a blueprint for what they should be looking at. Yes. Yes this section. More of this section.

and complies with some hesitation.

This makes it sound like he's obeying a command to be reluctant. I recommend changing this to 'it hesitantly complies,' or something along those lines.

Upon reaching the highest stable point he can reach

Redundant reach. I recommend changing this to 'Upon reaching the structure's apex,' or something like that.

The organisms approached SCP-2917, and appeared to charge at the researchers visible through it. A single creature traveled

You've been sticking with present tense up until this point, and continue in the present after this, so I'm going to assume these are typographical errors.

I'm going to follow the crowd and agree that this definitely needs some kind of escalation. I can respect a simple scientific study, but for the most part all we're treated to are these dull environments which don't really serve to emphasize the interesting facets of this entity. The bottom line is, it's well written enough, and some decent imagery, but it's all the surface of something that actually turns out to be rather boring. That's not to say I'm against it as a concept, but you don't do enough with it. That's the primary issue here; there's so much wasted potential, and with an idea that's fascinated Sci-Fi fans for generations and has even found its way into our little corner of the internet, in other incarnations.

tl;dr neutral vote, it's good in general but lacks any hitting power

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like… Scratch that, love, the idea of a relatively random wormhole leading to unknown and unknowable destinations in the night sky, not least of which because it reminds me of Stargate SG-1. That said, this thing almost feels too… Ordered… To be a natural event. I kinda feel like this is a leftover from some Prometheus experiment gone bad or someone finally blew up a stargate while it was running and it left this behind…

Either way, +1 for excellent use of mental imagery

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure how much better it is, but messed around for a while this afternoon in blender and photoshop and came up with this:

+ show block
– hide block

The 80's photo trashyness is hopefully mitigating the CGI here, but I've been staring at it for too long so y'all tell me if it's working or not.

Physics-wise, the effect was rendered using a little fancy material tomfoolery that nested a pair of invisible refraction lenses around a projected center image. In theory, it should be an accurate simulation of the gravity lensing you'd get from a naked singularity with a hole in the middle like you'd find in a wormhole.

Both landscapes are modified photos of my backyard I took.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've already started rewriting a few things based on criticism I've gotten here. The next revision should have a few extra tests, some useless tests cut out, 75% fewer dead D-Class, and a few niftier planets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm actually inclined to agree with you.

+11 after one day is a good upvote trajectory, so I was at least wrong about that. Whether you are satisfied with it is a different story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because I'm stupid. I'm gonna reorder some stuff a little, rewrite a few logs. That doesn't make any sense in-universe, and I need to fix it.

How does this thing count man+tether as two, but not man+space suit or man+air tank or whatever as two? It seems arbitrary when you intended it to be deterministic.

Man+Spacesuit? Container rule. Man+Air tank?… That's a solid point.

If this causes gravitational lensing sufficient to visibly distort spacetime as shown, how is Site-37 not shredded? Bending matter into pretzels happens long before light gets pretzeled.

*waves hands unconvincingly.* Rule of cool, I suppose.

I think this is going to be one of those ones that I just don't get why it is successful. My batting average on predicting success is taking a dive lately, between this and that dog pun article…

I'm actually inclined to agree with you. Other people's comments seem to be like yours - bland and unoriginal. I probably should've gotten more feedback before posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of hate to say it, but you're right. I'll see about improving it sometime - but frankly, real life is getting in my way for the next several days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It felt like it was building up to something, but ended on a weak note with the squid attack.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreeing with sirpudding and Petrograd, etc. This is by all means a technically well-written piece, but it's just so uncreative. The uninspiring test logs reiterate what is already summed up in the description, D-class are needlessly marched to their deaths, and it all just ends with mildly strange aliens. The use of "Safe Euclid" and similar strikethrough edits come-off as clumsy attempts at foreshadowing - I can't really understand why they even exist in a digital document, as by that logic, every other edit should be noted just as clearly.

In the end, this is just a wormhole played completely straight with no implications or insights about anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was playing around with something like this a couple of months ago. Great job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. The number of D class thrown across the galaxy and inadvertently abandoned beyond recall, less so.

The entity that charged through the wormhole and disappeared (from the other point of view) was an intriguing idea. I like to think that they're intelligent enough to bring word back to the others - "Yeah, there was this strange hole in the air, and we could see weird looking monsters through it (like this one we captured). Trogool ran at them and disappeared! The hole vanished after that."

item.title: Re: space travel issues
item.content:encoded: Risking contamination, like sending in giant bags of thriving bacteria as your first step, seems like bad scientific rigor, even if you are reasonably sure that contamination is unlikely.

item.title: Re: space travel issues
item.content:encoded:
We don't know, and the foundation is completely cautionless here.

It probably depends, ultimately, on if this is or is not the first contact with Extraterrestrial biospheres the Foundation has made. If it's not, then it can be surmised that the Foundation has decided that cross-contanination risks are so low as to be practically nonexistent. If it is first contact… yeah, the Foundation's taking major risks, at the very least when the living alien world popped up.

item.title: Re: space travel issues
item.content:encoded:
Speaking of disposable and unmanned probes, why not use drones?

Seconding this one. "Just throw D-Class at it" just does not seem like a great idea here.

item.title: Re: space travel issues
item.content:encoded:
All True. We don't know, and the foundation is completely cautionless here. It's lolfoundation, but without the laughs.

GeometryPrime, I don't mean to come off too harsh. It's well written, and I'm a sucker for space exploration. I would be eager to see a rewrite with more story to it. Why is the wormhole here? What's the connection among these planets? Is there a threat that forces the foundation to explore it despite the danger?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: according to google, french

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really nice. You've managed to turn a relatively well-known aspect of physics (a wormhole) into a well-executed and interesting SCP. This is very good for a first attempt, and it gets my upvote.

item.title: Re: space travel issues
item.content:encoded:
It's virtually impossible to prevent an unmanned probe from bringing Earth life to other planets, even when traveling years in space. NASA considers this a huge ethical concern for Europa, but the foundation is unconcerned?
…
An alien disease would be a new keter SCP in its own right.

To be fair, except for extremophiles, I don't think many microbes could survive in very different environmental conditions from Earth[[Footnote]]I'm aware of the bacteria found from recovered probes during the Apollo missions, but it should be noted that those went into hibernation in an attempt to wait for favorable conditions, if they could survive that long. [[/footnote]]. And that's assuming that the amino acids the local biosphete uses are compatible with the microbes to begin with.

This is all hypothetical until we encounter at leastvone alien biosphere, however, so real-world NASA has a good reason to be cautious rather than just assume.

item.title: space travel issues
item.content:encoded:
-1. Doesn't provide interesting perspective, scientific exploration, or a story.

My number one gripe here is biological contamination. It's virtually impossible to prevent an unmanned probe from bringing Earth life1 to other planets, even when traveling years in space. NASA considers this a huge ethical concern for Europa, but the foundation is unconcerned?

Related, Bringing the D-class back is more serious chance for contamination. Consider that Moon dust is hazardous2 and it's basically the same stuff as in Earth. An alien disease would be a new keter SCP in its own right. The potential to bring dangerous material justifies the disposable in D-class.

Speaking of disposable and unmanned probes, why not use drones? A bomb-disarming robot is cheap3 and nearly as useful as a D-class. This seems like a needless waste of D-classes.

Check out the other portal-exploration SCP for ideas. Usually, the locations are linked thematically and tell a story.

Footnotes
1. or the building blocks thereof
2. like asbestos.
3. cheap by foundation standards at least, certainly less than a space suit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I probably would have no voted if it weren't for the test logs. As it is, the test logs are the icing on the cake.
Take my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's working fine for me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't have time to read it right now but the image doesn't display for me. Anybody else seeing this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On a purely technical level, this is probably the best written wornhole skip I can think of

SCP-1322
SCP-2670
The Hydra's Spine

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tests are just weird. Why test if water can cross inside a container when they have already put people through?

How does this thing count man+tether as two, but not man+space suit or man+air tank or whatever as two? It seems arbitrary when you intended it to be deterministic.

If this causes gravitational lensing sufficient to visibly distort spacetime as shown, how is Site-37 not shredded? Bending matter into pretzels happens long before light gets pretzeled.

I think this is going to be one of those ones that I just don't get why it is successful. My batting average on predicting success is taking a dive lately, between this and that dog pun article…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Similarly to the others, I'm iffy about this.

+ show block
– hide block

From a tone and style standpoint, this is very well written. I actually like the picture.

But when you consider the story of this object, all that's present is "There is a wormhole to other worlds, with specific parameters. The Foundation has done testing to determine those parameters. One time a weird animal got through." The logs don't do much except for spell out details already known from the description and provide fairly bland alien vistas. The only notable action is the occasional loss of D-Class, and the only payoff you're building up to is the animal attack.

My usual critique is that logs that spell out how things already known in the description are a waste of space. Logs are for building tension, advancing the narrative, and adding interesting or evocative details. Your logs are well written, that's a strength for you, but they aren't in service of anything super-compelling. Other portals-to-worlds tell us interesting stories about those other worlds and the things or people in them (the red sea object, the window with red and blue men,), or the way the Foundationverse works (there's a "mirror" universe portal that turns out isn't quite a mirror), or the way the Foundation treats such things (the paddling pool). Stories can be about the actions of characters, or creators, or the Foundation, or even just the description of what the object does can be a story.

On a purely technical level, this is probably the best written wornhole skip I can think of, but sadly that just isn't as important. You've got to have a story you want to tell, and tell it in an interesting and intentional way. That, or imagery so wild or knocks people's socks off. This is really weak on both fronts.

So -1 from me. I like your writing and look forward to your future skips, but be sure to keep storytelling firmly in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing this. I'm not seeing much that separates this from other articles, and I get a feeling of deja vu, reading about another anomaly located in one specific location with spacetime properties.

Edit: the fuck is a deja vubin

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, it's not a reflective sphere.

It's a reasonably accurate simulation of a black hole that I edited into a wormhole.

I wasn't gonna use your typical sci-fi wormhole just because while it isn't a hard-science skip, I wasn't gonna pass up the chance to be as accurate as I could when it came to the picture - because that's pretty accurate gravitational lensing. I just couldn't do it to the planet on the other side like it would be IRL.

Although if you have a better image I'll totally use it - Mine is just a tad cheesy.

If it would help, I have a Black-and-White version of the same image, and I know black and white sometimes can help hide poorly-done special effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh, it seems to be working. You can't please me like 60% of the time.

Also, that image is obviously one of those 90's "Beyond the Mind's Eye" type CGI demo reel reflective sphere things and is totally cheesy. Is this wormhole at Radio Shack?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. I'm really not sure how I could address this without scrapping the whole thing though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, I still don't see what is distinguishing this from all the other traversable wormholes and interstellar portals and whatnot. This doesn't even work as a pure hard sf skip since the negative energy density required would make this not work on the Earth's surface (or at least it wouldn't be the Earth's surface for long). This is otherwise competent but really that isn't enough. So either I an missing something or this article is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Euclid is the most common classification in general, so simple probability would predict that at any given time you would likely observe this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite like this one, it has a really nice arch and just really makes a good scp, especially if this is your first.

Also, not a fault but I have noticed quite a few scp's lately have been classed Safe Euclid, or maybe it's just me picking up on this I'm not sure.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A wormhole as a SCP. Well done. +1

item.title: SCP-2917
item.content:encoded:
I've never written an SCP before, and frankly I'm a bit nervous to put this up for judgment, but I'm pretty sure there's no point where I wouldn't be.

Many thanks to the people on the forum and on Reddit who gave me some critique!

Edit2: I rewrote the test logs. I took some of your criticisms into account, and I realized I had two main goals - to describe some interesting planets and to explain the specific properties of the wormhole. This led to two major changes. First, all the old planet descriptions were scrapped. Second, there are now two experiment logs - a "test log" describing experimentation into how the object works, as well as an "exploration log" describing the actual planets the Foundation explores through the anomaly. I also added an event at the end, to try and showcase some of the dangers this object might hold despite existing containment procedures.
